worth_it_explanation: The Insight Tag runs in the browser, so the conversions it records depend on a cookie surviving from the ad click to the outcome. Ad blockers and cookie restrictions remove a share of those records, and offline outcomes such as a closed deal never reach the browser at all. The Conversions API sends conversions from a server or a CRM system, so it reports outcomes the tag drops and outcomes that happen away from the site. These checks are quick to run inside Campaign Manager, and they catch two failures that cost the account either way: no server-side path at all, and a server-side path that double-counts because it shares no deduplication key with the tag.
intro: The LinkedIn Conversions API (CAPI) sends conversions to LinkedIn from a server or a customer relationship management (CRM) system rather than from the browser, so it complements the browser-side Insight Tag and can report offline outcomes such as a closed deal. Because the server-side path does not rely on a cookie, it reports conversions that browsers drop after the ad click. These checks confirm whether a server-side or offline conversion path exists, and whether it shares a deduplication key with the Insight Tag so the same conversion is not counted twice.

audit_checks.title: The Conversions API is not implemented, so server-side and offline signals are missing
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the account leans entirely on the browser-side Insight Tag, conversions that browsers drop after the ad click go unreported, and offline outcomes such as a closed deal never reach LinkedIn at all. The reported conversion volume runs lower than the real performance, which can understate which campaigns are working and hold back budget on them.

educate.base:
The LinkedIn Conversions API (CAPI) sends conversions to LinkedIn from a server or a customer relationship management (CRM) system instead of from the browser. Because the data does not depend on a cookie that has to survive from the ad click to the outcome, the server-side path reports conversions that ad blockers and browser cookie restrictions remove from the Insight Tag. It can also report offline outcomes, such as a lead that turns into a closed deal weeks later, which the browser never sees.

Without a server-side or CRM-based conversion path, the account leans entirely on the Insight Tag. The conversions the tag drops stay uncounted, and the offline value that often matters most on LinkedIn, given its business-to-business (B2B) audience, has no way to reach LinkedIn at all. The reported volume then runs lower than the real performance, and the campaigns that are actually driving outcomes can look weaker than they are.

You may want to confirm whether any server-side or offline path feeds conversions into Campaign Manager. A few signs point to the Conversions API being absent:
  • Campaign Manager shows conversion sources from the Insight Tag only, with no API or CRM source listed
  • Offline outcomes, such as closed deals from the CRM, never appear as conversions
  • No direct integration or LinkedIn Marketing Partner connection (e.g., Zapier or a tag manager) is set up to post conversions
LinkedIn offers two ways to implement the Conversions API. You can build a direct integration against the conversions endpoint, or you can connect through a LinkedIn Marketing Partner such as Zapier or a server-side tag manager. Either way, the goal is to add a server-side source so the account is not relying on the browser alone.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, especially a considered or B2B purchase where the buyer researches before converting, the Conversions API can report the order from the server even when the browser dropped the cookie between the click and the checkout. You may want to confirm the purchase event reaches LinkedIn from the server, rather than from the Insight Tag alone.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, which is the dominant case on LinkedIn, the value often lands offline. A form fill becomes a qualified lead, then a closed deal, in the CRM rather than on the site. You may want to confirm the CRM posts those downstream outcomes to LinkedIn through the Conversions API, so the account optimizes toward real revenue instead of raw form fills.

investigate.intro: Confirm in Campaign Manager whether any conversion is sourced from a server or CRM through the Conversions API, rather than from the Insight Tag alone.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager and select the ad account you are auditing.
Open 'Analyze', then 'Conversion tracking', to list the account's conversion rules.
Open each conversion rule and note its data source. A rule fed by the Conversions API shows a server or API source, often alongside a partner integration name, rather than the Insight Tag alone.
If every rule reports the Insight Tag as its only source, and no offline or CRM outcomes appear as conversions, record that the Conversions API is not implemented.

crm_or_server_logs.label: CRM or server records
steps: Check whether the CRM or the server that records conversions has any outbound connection to LinkedIn, such as a direct API integration or a partner connector (e.g., Zapier).
Confirm whether downstream outcomes, such as a closed deal, are sent to LinkedIn as conversions or only kept inside the CRM.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per conversion rule in Campaign Manager, whether its source includes a server or CRM path through the Conversions API. An account that posts at least its primary conversions, and any offline outcomes, from the server passes. An account whose conversion rules all report the Insight Tag as the only source is a fail recorded with a note on which outcomes are missing a server-side path.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the data source for each conversion rule above, and whether offline outcomes reach LinkedIn, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} relies on the browser-side Insight Tag alone, with no server-side or customer relationship management (CRM) path through the LinkedIn Conversions API. Conversions that browsers drop after the ad click go unreported, and offline outcomes such as a closed deal never reach LinkedIn, so the reported volume runs lower than the real performance and can understate which campaigns are working. {{voice_recommend}} adding a Conversions API source, either as a direct integration or through a LinkedIn Marketing Partner, so primary conversions and downstream offline outcomes are sent from the server. After the change, confirm in Campaign Manager that the conversion rules show a server or API source and that offline outcomes appear as conversions.

audit_checks.title: The Conversions API sends events without deduplication against the Insight Tag
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the Insight Tag and the Conversions API both report the same conversion without a shared event identifier, LinkedIn counts the conversion twice. The reported volume runs higher than the real performance, and Smart Bidding and budget decisions then optimize toward an inflated number.

educate.base:
When a conversion is reported through both the Insight Tag and the Conversions API (CAPI), LinkedIn needs a way to recognize that the two records describe the same event. That way is a shared event identifier. If the browser-side event and the server-side event carry the same event ID, LinkedIn discards the duplicate and counts the conversion once. Without a matching event ID, the two records look like two separate conversions, so the same outcome is counted twice.

LinkedIn deduplicates on the event ID. When it receives an Insight Tag event and a Conversions API event from the same account with the same event ID, it discards the Conversions API event and keeps the Insight Tag event in reporting. For this to work, the account needs a separate conversion rule for each data source, the browser and the server, and the server-side event has to carry the same event ID the browser assigned.

For page-load conversions, the event ID is set on the browser by assigning it to the '_linkedin_event_id' property on the global window namespace before the Insight Tag base code runs. The server then reads that same value and sends it with the matching event. You may want to confirm that a single event ID is generated once per conversion and passed to both paths, rather than each path minting its own.

A practical check on whether deduplication is working is the count itself. Because LinkedIn removes the redundant Conversions API events, the Insight Tag conversion count is usually higher than the Conversions API count when deduplication is set up correctly. Two paths reporting similar, additive totals can be a sign the event IDs are not matching.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the purchase fires on the Insight Tag at checkout and is often also posted from the server for resilience. You may want to confirm both the browser-side purchase and the server-side purchase carry the same event ID, so a single order is not counted as two.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, a form submission may fire on the Insight Tag and also be posted from the server or CRM. You may want to confirm the same event ID rides both paths, so one lead does not inflate the conversion count as two.

investigate.intro: Confirm in Campaign Manager whether any conversion is reported through both the Insight Tag and the Conversions API, and whether both paths carry a matching event ID.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: Open Campaign Manager, select the ad account, and open 'Analyze', then 'Conversion tracking'.

Identify any conversion that is reported through both the Insight Tag and the Conversions API. LinkedIn's guidance is to use a separate conversion rule per data source, so look for browser and server rules that describe the same outcome.

Compare the recent counts for the paired rules. If deduplication is working, the Insight Tag count is usually higher than the Conversions API count because the redundant API events are removed. Similar additive totals can point to event IDs that are not matching.

tag_inspector.label: Browser tag inspector
steps: Load a page that fires a conversion and inspect the Insight Tag event to find the event ID it assigns, for example the value placed on the '_linkedin_event_id' window property.
Confirm with the engineering or measurement owner that the server reads that same event ID and sends it with the matching Conversions API event, rather than generating a new one server-side.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per conversion reported through both paths, whether the browser-side and server-side events carry a matching event ID. A paired conversion where both paths share one event ID, and where the Insight Tag count runs higher than the Conversions API count, passes. A paired conversion with no shared event ID, or with additive counts that suggest no deduplication, is a fail recorded with the conversion name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each conversion reported through both the Insight Tag and the Conversions API, whether a matching event ID is passed, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} reports conversions through both the Insight Tag and the LinkedIn Conversions API without a shared event identifier. LinkedIn deduplicates on a matching event ID, so when the browser-side and server-side events do not carry the same ID, the same conversion is counted twice, and the reported volume runs higher than the real performance. Bidding and budget decisions then optimize toward an inflated number. {{voice_recommend}} generating one event ID per conversion on the browser and passing that same value with the server-side event, so LinkedIn collapses the duplicate. After the change, confirm in Campaign Manager that the Insight Tag count runs higher than the Conversions API count for the paired conversions, which indicates deduplication is working.
